docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
• v0.2 · April 2026 — Revised to match RFC-001 v0.2's flat schema. Replaced "loaded_yaml.creative / loaded_yaml.technical" partitioning with "lines below marker — creative-state keys + technical blocks (render, audio)". Added open question about marker-comment recovery on accidental deletion. Updated trade-offs to note the dual-source-of-truth between creative-state YAML keys and derived blocks.
  • v0.1 · April 2026 — Initial draft, written assuming the two-tier (creative: / technical:) schema from RFC-001 v0.1. Superseded by v0.2.

docs.title: RFC-006 — Audio system design
docs.text: Status · Decided · closed 2026-05-03 → ADR-026 TA anchor · §components/render-system · §contracts (audio scalars in payload) Related · PRD-005 Video Editor · RFC-002 Render payload format · RFC-007 Key moment detection Closes into · ADR-026 Why this is an RFC · The audio system is the most ambitious piece of OceanCanvas's enrichment surface, and the one where "decoration vs. data" is hardest to get right (PRD-005 sharpest threat). Multiple plausible architectures exist with real trade-offs around fidelity, dependency cost, and authorship. This is the meatiest RFC in the system.

docs.title: The question
docs.text:
A timelapse video assembles a recipe's accumulated PNG renders into film. Music plays over the film. The music must:

  1. Sound like it belongs to the data, not to a stock library.
  2. React to the data's behaviour — calmer in stable periods, intensifying as anomalies build, peaking when records break.
  3. Honour the determinism principle — the same recipe + same date range + same audio settings should produce the same audio track.
  4. Be exportable in standard MP4 containers without exotic tooling.

How do we generate this music, and how do we drive it from the data scalar time series?

Three plausible architectures:

  1. Commercial generative music API (Mubert, Beatoven, etc.) with stem-based crossfading driven by data.
  2. Custom synthesis using a web-audio library (Tone.js, Web Audio API) with explicit synthesis driven by data.
  3. Pre-recorded stem library authored by the project, with crossfading between intensity levels driven by data.

Each trades off authorship, dependency cost, fidelity, and licensing differently. The choice is consequential — it affects the project's character, its cost structure, and its long-term sustainability.

docs.title: Use cases
docs.text:
1. Default theme on a year-long timelapse. Artist exports a 30-second film of a year of SST data. Picks the "ambient" theme. Music should swell at anomalies, calm at stable periods, peak at the record day, end gracefully.
  2. Dramatic theme on a sea-level rise timelapse. Artist picks "dramatic" for a 30-year sea-level film. Music should build cumulatively across the rise, with marked moments at El Niño / La Niña events.
  3. Silent export. Artist disables audio entirely. Film exports without an audio track.
  4. Remix / re-export. Artist exports the same film with a different theme, comparing how the data feels under different musical interpretations.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• Music is driven by the data, audibly. A listener can hear the connection between visual events and audio events.
  • The same scalar time series produces the same music every time (determinism).
  • Themes are distinct — ambient, dramatic, ethereal sound recognisably different.
  • The system is self-hostable end-to-end (no required cloud dependency that breaks on offline use). A graceful "no audio" fallback works.
  • Audio export uses standard MP4-compatible codecs.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• Determinism — same inputs → same audio bytes (TA §constraints). Generative APIs that introduce per-call randomness must be seedable or cached.
  • Self-hostable — the system must work without commercial APIs as a fallback (TA §constraints).
  • Daily clock — audio is generated at export time, not pipeline time. The pipeline produces frames; the video editor produces film + audio (TA §constraints).
  • Attribution baked in — music attribution travels with the export. Both the data sources and the audio source/license must be in the bundled metadata.

docs.title: Proposed approach
docs.text:
A two-tier system: stem-based crossfading as the primary architecture, with a pluggable stem source.

Each theme is a set of pre-rendered stems at four to five intensity levels. The video editor reads the per-frame data scalar (RFC-007 produces this), maps it to an intensity value 0.0–1.0, and crossfades between the stems for the playback duration.

Theme: "ambient"
  ├── stem_0_calm.wav        (lowest intensity, sparse)
  ├── stem_1_breathing.wav   (low-mid, gentle build)
  ├── stem_2_present.wav     (mid, full texture)
  ├── stem_3_swelling.wav    (high, peak texture)
  └── stem_4_apex.wav        (highest, record-moment)

At export, ffmpeg crossfades between the appropriate stems at each frame. The crossfade ratio is determined by the per-frame intensity from RFC-007. A statistical peak in the data lifts the intensity → the next stem is crossed in. A record value pushes intensity to maximum → stem_4 dominates.

Where do the stems come from? The pluggable stem source has two implementations:

Implementation A: locally-rendered stems. Five canonical themes ship with the project as .wav files in audio/themes/{theme_name}/. Free and open (Creative Commons or similar). Self-contained — the system works fully offline.

Implementation B: commercial API stem generation. When a user wants a custom theme not in the local library, the editor calls Mubert (or equivalent) with the theme parameters. The API returns the five stems; they are cached locally per theme. Subsequent uses of that theme on the same machine use the cache (deterministic).

The stem-based approach decouples music generation (slow, sometimes external) from music playback driven by data (fast, always local). The data-driven part of the system is fully deterministic and self-hostable; the music itself can be sourced from anywhere.

For sub-frame transitions (smooth crossfades between intensity levels rather than discrete steps), ffmpeg's acrossfade filter or a per-frame mixing pass handles the interpolation.

docs.title: Alternative: direct commercial API for full track
docs.text:
Each export calls Mubert or similar with the data scalar as a control input, receives a complete track for the export.

Rejected because (a) it makes the system depend on a commercial API for any music output — breaking the self-hostable constraint; (b) determinism is hard to guarantee through an external API; (c) cost scales linearly with exports. The stem approach uses the API only at theme-creation time, then caches.

docs.title: Alternative: custom synthesis (Tone.js / Web Audio API)
docs.text:
Generate music live in the browser using a web-audio synthesis stack with explicit data-driven envelopes.

Rejected because the authorship cost is enormous. Writing engaging music for five themes from synthesis primitives is a multi-month effort outside the scope of v1, and the result is unlikely to be musically strong without dedicated composer involvement. Stem-based crossfading uses pre-composed music and lets the data drive the arrangement, which is a more tractable design problem.

docs.title: Alternative: silent video only, audio added externally
docs.text:
Defer audio entirely. Films export silent; users can add audio in their own editing tools.

Rejected because the audio is a core part of the enrichment promise of PRD-005. A silent timelapse is a different product. Acceptable as a fallback (Use case 3 above) but not as the default.

docs.title: Alternative: a single track per theme, not stems
docs.text:
Each theme is one audio file, with envelope modulation controlled by data. Simpler than stems.

Rejected because envelope modulation alone cannot create the qualitative shifts between "calm" and "swelling" textures that the use cases require. A single track played louder is not the same as a swelling track. Five stems give the system five qualitatively distinct sonic states to interpolate between.

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
• Stem authoring is up-front work. The five canonical themes ship as audio files that someone has to create (compose, record, master). This is real labour and the quality bar matters. Mitigation: source from a commercial API to bootstrap, then iterate or replace.
  • Crossfade artefacts. Crossfading between musical stems can produce phase or harmonic artefacts. Acceptable at low frequency (one crossfade per several seconds). Mitigation: design themes such that adjacent intensity stems are harmonically related.
  • Cache invalidation. A user re-generating a theme via API may get a different result on two different machines. Determinism requires the cache to be authoritative; document that themes are per-user-machine until shared.
  • License attribution. Every theme stem carries a license; the export must bundle this. Add to the citation file requirement.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
1. Which commercial API for Implementation B? Mubert and Beatoven both have generative-music APIs with similar capabilities. Need a separate ADR to evaluate (pricing, terms, output quality, license terms).
  2. How many intensity levels per theme — four, five, or six? More levels = smoother crossfades but more stems to author. Probably five for v1; revisit.
  3. What's the data-scalar-to-intensity mapping function? Direct (anomaly = intensity) is simple; statistically normalised (z-score → intensity) is more robust across different data ranges. RFC-007 territory; decide jointly.
  4. Does the artist control the energy arc explicitly (as PRD-005 suggests), or is it derived purely from data? Probably both — artist sets a "responsiveness" parameter, data drives within that envelope.

docs.title: How this closes
docs.text:
• ADR-NNN — Audio system architecture. Locks the stem-based crossfading approach.
  • ADR-NNN — Stem source policy. Locks the local-first / API-fallback model.
  • ADR-NNN — Commercial API choice for theme generation. Picks Mubert vs. Beatoven vs. another, with a fallback strategy if the chosen vendor changes terms.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• TA — §components/render-system · §contracts (audio scalars in payload) · §constraints (determinism, self-hostable)
  • Related PRDs — PRD-005 Video Editor (this RFC was flagged in its open threads)
  • Related RFCs — RFC-002 Render payload format · RFC-007 Key moment detection

docs.title: RFC-007 — Key moment detection
docs.text: Status · Decided · closed 2026-05-03 → ADR-024 TA anchor · §components/render-system · §contracts (audio scalars in payload) Related · PRD-005 Video Editor · RFC-006 Audio system Closes into · ADR-024 Why this is an RFC · Audio swells and overlay markers must fire at the same frames — when the music swells, the record-flash appears, the anomaly bar peaks. PRD-005's sharpest threat (audio reads as decorative) is defeated specifically when the visual and audio events are demonstrably synchronised. They share a single per-frame intensity signal. The algorithm that produces that signal is open: multiple plausible approaches, each detecting different kinds of "moment."

docs.title: The question
docs.text:
Given a data scalar time series — say, daily SST anomaly for the North Atlantic over a year — produce a per-frame intensity signal that captures moments worth marking. The signal is consumed by:

  • Audio (RFC-006): the per-frame intensity drives crossfading between musical stems.
  • Overlays: the intensity threshold determines when a record-flash, anomaly-grow, or named-event annotation appears.

What constitutes "a moment worth marking"? Several plausible signals exist:

  1. Statistical peaks — values exceeding the mean by more than 2 standard deviations.
  2. Record values — values exceeding all prior values in the time series.
  3. Threshold crossings — values crossing a user-defined or scientifically-meaningful boundary (e.g., bleaching threshold for SST).
  4. Inflection points — moments where the rate of change reverses or accelerates significantly.

Each captures a different kind of moment. A complete system uses all four, combined into one intensity signal. The deliberation is the combination — how to weight, scale, and merge them into a single 0.0–1.0 value per frame.

docs.title: Use cases
docs.text:
1. Year of SST anomaly. Statistical peaks fire mid-summer (above-mean warming days). Records fire in late August (warmest day ever). Threshold crossings fire on the days exceeding the 1.5°C warming line. Inflection points fire at the start and end of heat-wave periods.
  2. Decadal sea-level rise. Records fire constantly in recent years (every month is a new high). Statistical peaks barely fire (the trend dominates the noise). Threshold crossings on integer-cm marks. Inflection points on acceleration changes.
  3. Year of sea-ice extent. Records fire at the September minimum. Statistical peaks rarely fire (seasonal signal dominates). Threshold crossings at scientifically-meaningful extents. Inflection points at freeze-up and break-up dates.
  4. A calm time series. No records, few peaks, no significant inflections. Intensity stays low; audio remains calm; overlays don't intrude.

The four use cases want different relative weights on the four signals. A unified algorithm with sensible defaults plus per-recipe tuning is the goal.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• One algorithm, one signal, both audio and overlays consume the same value for the same frame.
  • The signal is deterministic — same time series produces the same per-frame intensities.
  • The signal is calm-by-default — without significant events, intensity stays low.
  • The signal scales gracefully across different data ranges (SST anomaly vs. sea-level vs. ice extent).
  • The four signal types are inspectable individually (the editor can show which kind of moment was detected, for debugging and overlay labelling).

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• Determinism — same time series + same parameters → same intensity signal (TA §constraints).
  • Single source of truth for moments — audio and overlays compute from the same algorithm output. No separate moment-detection paths (TA §constraints).
  • No machine learning models in v1 — the algorithm must be classical (statistical, threshold-based). Trainable models are deferred.

docs.title: Proposed approach
docs.text:
Four detectors, each producing an intensity contribution; a weighted sum, clamped to 0.0–1.0.

For each frame f in a time series of values v[0..N]:

peaks_score(f)        = abs(v[f] - mean) / (k * stddev)        # k=2 for the 2σ convention
records_score(f)      = 1.0 if v[f] >= max(v[0..f-1]) else 0.0
threshold_score(f)    = 1.0 if v[f] crosses a defined threshold this frame else 0.0
inflection_score(f)   = abs(d²v/dt² at f) normalised to [0, 1]

intensity(f) = clamp(
  w_peaks      * peaks_score(f)      +
  w_records    * records_score(f)    +
  w_threshold  * threshold_score(f)  +
  w_inflection * inflection_score(f),
  0.0, 1.0
)

The weights w_* are configurable per recipe (or per export), with sensible defaults:

# default key_moment weights
weights:
  peaks: 0.4
  records: 0.3
  threshold: 0.2
  inflection: 0.1

Records get higher weight than inflections because record events are the most viscerally meaningful in climate-communicator use cases.

The intensity signal is temporally smoothed with a small window (e.g., 3-frame Gaussian) before being consumed, so that a single anomalous frame produces a brief audio swell rather than a one-frame click.

Each detector also produces a labelled event when its score crosses a high threshold (>0.7). The overlay system uses the labels to choose annotation type:

  • High peaks_score → "anomaly indicator grows"
  • High records_score → "record flash" + label "highest [variable] ever measured"
  • High threshold_score → "named event marker"
  • High inflection_score → "trend change" subtle marker

Same signal drives audio crossfading; the labels drive overlay choice.

docs.title: Alternative: just use anomaly directly
docs.text:
Define intensity(f) = abs(anomaly(f)) normalised to [0, 1]. Skip the four-detector composition.

Rejected because anomaly alone misses records (a value 1.5σ above mean that is also a record is more meaningful than a 2σ value that is unremarkable in a long record). Records and anomalies are different kinds of significance, and the system should treat them differently. The composition is a small cost for a meaningful gain in expressiveness.

docs.title: Alternative: ML-based event detection
docs.text:
Train a model on labelled "interesting moments" in time series; use the model output as the intensity signal.

Rejected for v1. Adds a model-training pipeline, model versioning, dataset curation — entire infrastructure. The classical four-detector approach handles the use cases cleanly. Revisit if classical approach proves insufficient.

docs.title: Alternative: single threshold per recipe
docs.text:
Set one threshold; intensity is binary (event / no event).

Rejected because audio crossfading needs continuous values, not binary. Overlays could use binary, but coupling audio and overlays through a single signal forces continuous output.

docs.title: Alternative: data-source-specific algorithms
docs.text:
Different algorithms for SST, sea level, ice extent — tuned to each variable's typical statistics.

Rejected because the four-detector model with configurable weights handles per-source tuning at the weights level rather than the algorithm level. Keeps the algorithm uniform across sources; just the parameters change.

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
• Default weights are a choice that won't be right for every recipe. Mitigated by per-recipe overrides; documented as expected.
  • Smoothing window is a magic number. 3-frame is conservative; longer smoothing makes audio mushier; shorter makes audio jittery. Tuneable.
  • The intensity signal is a single channel. A more nuanced design might output multiple channels (peaks-channel, records-channel, etc.) to drive multi-instrument arrangements. Reserved for a future iteration.
  • Inflection detection is sensitive to noise. Second derivatives amplify noise. Mitigated by smoothing the time series before computing the inflection score.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
1. Is the threshold-crossing detector worth the complexity? It requires per-recipe configuration of meaningful thresholds. Default to "off" for recipes that don't define them?
  2. How does this interact with multi-source recipes? Currently the algorithm assumes one scalar time series. A composite recipe with two sources may want to combine their signals — additively, multiplicatively, or pick the dominant one?
  3. Should the algorithm produce its output once at export time, or per-frame on demand during preview? Probably once at export, cached. Preview can use a cheap approximation.
  4. Storage: do we store the per-frame intensity signal as part of the export bundle, or recompute every time? Storing makes the export reproducible without re-running the algorithm; recomputing keeps the bundle small.

docs.title: How this closes
docs.text: • ADR-NNN — Key moment detection algorithm. Locks the four-detector composition, the default weights, the smoothing window, and the per-recipe override mechanism.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• TA — §components/render-system · §contracts (audio scalars)
  • Related PRDs — PRD-005 Video Editor
  • Related RFCs — RFC-006 Audio system (consumes this signal)

docs.title: RFC-008 — Pipeline Parallelisation Strategy
docs.text: Status · Decided · closed 2026-05-02 → ADR-023 TA anchor · §pipeline/tasks · §constraints Related · OC-04 §six-pipeline-tasks · OC-02 §creative-loop · RFC-009 (CLI) Closes into · ADR-023 Why this is an RFC · The daily pipeline and historical backfill both have parallelisable stages, but the right concurrency model — and its safe upper bound on this hardware — is not yet decided. Choosing the wrong model (e.g. threading for CPU-bound work, or uncapped Chromium instances) risks correctness failures or RAM exhaustion. The answer requires measuring actual render time and Chromium memory footprint before it can be locked.

docs.title: The question
docs.text:
The current pipeline design is implicitly sequential: one source fetched, one source processed, one recipe rendered at a time. For the daily run with a small recipe count this is acceptable. For the historical backfill — up to 540 images at monthly cadence, or ~16,400 at daily cadence — sequential execution is the bottleneck that determines whether backfill is a one-hour job or a multi-day job.

The pipeline's six tasks split cleanly into two groups: tasks that are serial by dependency (Discover must precede Fetch; Index must follow all Renders), and tasks that are embarrassingly parallel within a stage (each source's Fetch is independent; each recipe's Render is independent). The question is how to exploit that independence safely and with what concurrency primitives, given this machine: an Apple MacBook Pro with many cores and 48 GB RAM.

Two sub-questions compound the main one. First, the right concurrency model differs by task type: I/O-bound work (Fetch) benefits from async or threading; CPU-bound work with subprocess isolation (Render via Puppeteer) benefits from process-level parallelism. Second, the safe upper bound on simultaneous Chromium instances is unknown until measured — too many and RAM pressure causes page-outs that make parallelism slower than sequential.

docs.title: Use cases
docs.text:
Daily run. Multiple recipes accumulate over time. Rendering ten recipes sequentially at ~15s each = 2.5 minutes. Rendering them in parallel at a concurrency of 8 = ~20 seconds. The gap widens as recipe count grows.

Historical backfill. 540 monthly frames × ~12s = ~1.8 hours sequential. At concurrency 8 = ~14 minutes. This is the case that makes parallelism non-optional if the backfill is to be a practical one-session operation.

Recipe preview in editor. The live preview in the recipe editor is a single render triggered on demand. Parallelism does not apply here, but the render function's interface must be compatible with both single and batched invocation.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
The parallelisation strategy must:

  • reduce backfill wall-clock time to under 30 minutes for the 540-image monthly dataset
  • keep peak RAM below 32 GB to leave 16 GB headroom for the OS and other processes
  • integrate with Prefect's existing @task / @flow model without replacing it
  • preserve Prefect's result caching — a date already rendered must never be re-rendered
  • leave the render(recipe, date) → png_path function signature pure and stateless, so it works identically in daily, backfill, and preview contexts

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
From OC-04 §constraints:

  • Orchestration is Prefect. The task runner is the correct insertion point for concurrency; the task functions themselves stay unchanged.
  • Deployment target is Docker Compose on this machine. No distributed workers, no Kubernetes.
  • Chromium instances are spawned by Puppeteer inside the pipeline container. Each instance carries an estimated 200–400 MB RSS. The actual figure must be measured in Slice 1.
  • The pipeline container runs Python + Node + Chromium. The concurrency limit must be a configurable constant, not hard-coded, so it can be tuned per host.

docs.title: Proposed approach
docs.text: Apply ConcurrentTaskRunner at the flow level and use Prefect's .submit() with a bounded semaphore for Task 05 (Render). Concurrency is controlled by a single constant RENDER_CONCURRENCY in pipeline config, defaulting to 6 pending measurement.

docs.title: Chromium worker model
docs.text:
Each parallel render worker owns one persistent Chromium instance for the duration of its work. The instance is spawned once when the worker starts and kept alive across all renders assigned to that worker — load payload → render → screenshot → load next payload → repeat. The instance is closed only when the worker exits.

This eliminates per-frame Chromium startup cost (estimated 3–5s of a 12–15s render). The semaphore in the proposed approach caps workers, not individual render calls. RENDER_CONCURRENCY = 6 means six Chromium instances alive simultaneously, each processing its queue of assigned frames sequentially.

# Sketch — not implementation spec
RENDER_CONCURRENCY = 6  # caps workers (= Chromium instances), not frames

@flow(task_runner=ConcurrentTaskRunner())
def daily_pipeline():
    sources  = discover.map(ALL_SOURCES)
    fetched  = fetch.map(sources)
    processed = process.map(fetched)

    payloads = build_payload.map(ALL_RECIPES, wait_for=[processed])
    renders  = render.map(ALL_RECIPES, wait_for=[payloads])
    index.submit(wait_for=renders)

@flow(task_runner=ConcurrentTaskRunner())
def backfill_pipeline(recipe, start_date, end_date, cadence):
    dates   = generate_dates(start_date, end_date, cadence)
    renders = render.map([(recipe, d) for d in dates])
    index.submit(wait_for=renders)

docs.title: Task-by-task breakdown
docs.text:
Task 02 — Fetch. Fan out across sources using .submit(). I/O-bound; threading is appropriate. Modest gain at three Phase 1 sources but costs nothing and scales as sources are added.

Task 03 — Process. Fan out across sources using .submit(). I/O-bound at the read step, briefly CPU-bound at the colormap step. Parallel across sources; per-date processing stays sequential within a source to keep file writes predictable.

Task 04 — Build Payload. Fan out across recipes using .submit(). Fast Python work; parallelism here eliminates the last sequential loop before render.

Task 05 — Render. Fan out across recipes (daily) or across dates (backfill) using .submit() with a semaphore capped at RENDER_CONCURRENCY. Each slot holds one persistent Chromium worker. Prefect result caching ensures already-rendered PNGs are skipped before the semaphore is acquired.

Task 06 — Index. Unchanged. Runs after all render futures resolve. Fast directory walk; no parallelism needed.

docs.title: Alternatives considered
docs.text:
ProcessPoolExecutor outside Prefect. Would work for render parallelism but bypasses Prefect's result caching, retry logic, and observability. Every render would be opaque to the Prefect UI. Rejected: losing caching and retry is worse than any performance gain.

Async Puppeteer (Node worker threads). Running Puppeteer in async Node threads rather than separate Chromium processes would reduce overhead but introduces a Node concurrency model inside a Python task runner. Rejected at this scale; revisit if profiling shows Chromium inter-process overhead dominates.

Uncapped concurrency. Removing the semaphore entirely. With 540 backfill dates and fast .submit() calls this would spawn hundreds of Chromium instances simultaneously. At 300 MB/instance × 100 instances = 30 GB, saturating available memory. Rejected.

Docker child container workers. Spawning a worker container per render job, orchestrated from a parent container. Achieves the same parallelism as the internal model but adds Docker socket complexity, sibling container networking, and shared volume coordination. The internal ConcurrentTaskRunner + semaphore model achieves equivalent throughput on single-machine hardware without that surface area. Rejected.

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
Parallel renders inside one container share the container's renders/ volume. File write contention is not expected — render output paths are non-overlapping per recipe per date — but must be confirmed during Slice 1 backfill testing.

ConcurrentTaskRunner uses Python threads, not processes. This is correct for I/O-bound tasks (Fetch) and for Render (where CPU work is inside Chromium subprocesses, not Python threads). If Task 03 (Process) proves slow under profiling, it should be moved to a ProcessPoolExecutor independently.

The persistent Chromium worker model means a crashed Chromium instance takes down its worker slot for the remainder of the run. Prefect's per-task timeout (timeout_seconds) mitigates runaway hangs; a crashed instance is logged and the slot is retired gracefully, with its unfinished frames failing and surfaced for re-run via oc render.

RENDER_CONCURRENCY must be measured, not guessed. The value of 6 is a starting point. Slice 1 empirical work sets the real ceiling.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
All design questions are resolved. Two items remain as Slice 1 empirical follow-ups, not blocking decisions:

  1. Chromium RSS in practice. Measure actual peak RSS per Chromium instance (field render vs particles render) during early Slice 1 using psutil or docker stats. Result sets the true safe ceiling for RENDER_CONCURRENCY. ADR-NNN (render concurrency limit) closes once this is measured.

  2. File write contention and semaphore placement. Confirm absence of filesystem contention under parallel writes during first backfill test. Confirm whether the semaphore lives more cleanly inside the task function (asyncio.Semaphore) or as a Prefect flow-level concurrency limit. Both are implementation details resolved by doing.

docs.title: How this closes
docs.text:
ADR-NNN — Prefect task runner and concurrency model. Locks ConcurrentTaskRunner + .submit() and the persistent Chromium worker model. Closes early in Slice 1 once the implementation shape is confirmed.

ADR-NNN — Render concurrency limit. Locks RENDER_CONCURRENCY value once Chromium RSS is measured. Closes at end of Slice 1 after backfill smoke test.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• OC-04 Technical Architecture — §six-pipeline-tasks, §constraints, §tech-stack
  • OC-02 Project Concept — §creative-loop (step 05 Accumulate)
  • RFC-009 — CLI Interface (backfill flow entry point: oc backfill)
  • Prefect docs — ConcurrentTaskRunner, .submit(), flow-level concurrency limits

docs.title: RFC-009 — Pipeline CLI Interface
docs.text: Status · Decided · closed 2026-05-02 → ADR-022 TA anchor · §pipeline/tasks · §deployment · §constraints Related · OC-04 §six-pipeline-tasks · OC-04 §deployment · RFC-008 (parallelisation) Closes into · ADR-022 Why this is an RFC · The pipeline currently has no defined entry point for ad-hoc invocation. The Prefect server handles the scheduled daily run, but there is no sanctioned way to trigger a backfill, re-render a single date, or inspect pipeline state without going through Docker internals or the Prefect UI directly. Adding a CLI requires deciding how it relates to the Prefect server — whether it calls flows directly, dispatches through the server, or both — and that relationship determines how observability is split across invocation modes. This is a genuine design question with real trade-offs between simplicity and visibility.

docs.title: The question
docs.text:
The pipeline runs on a daily schedule via Prefect Server inside Docker Compose. That covers the production cadence, but it leaves a gap: there is no clean way to invoke the pipeline outside that schedule. Triggering a backfill, re-rendering a failed date, validating a new recipe, or inspecting what has and has not been rendered all require either navigating the Prefect UI, running docker compose exec commands, or writing ad-hoc scripts.

A CLI closes that gap. The design question is how the CLI relates to the Prefect Server. Prefect flows can be invoked in two modes: directly as Python functions (the @flow decorator works without a running server — caching and retries still operate via the filesystem), or dispatched to the server (the run appears in the UI, is logged to Prefect's database, and is observable in the dashboard at localhost:4200).

These two modes have different characteristics. Direct invocation is simpler, always available, and gives immediate terminal feedback. Server dispatch provides structured run history, task-level timing, and error traces in the Prefect UI — the observability layer that makes the daily production run legible over time. The RFC proposes a design that uses both, with the invocation mode as an explicit flag rather than a hidden implementation detail.

docs.title: Use cases
docs.text:
Backfill. Running the 45-year historical dataset (RFC-008) is the primary motivation. The user needs to invoke backfill with a recipe name, date range, and cadence, then watch progress in the terminal. This is a long-running job — up to 30 minutes for 540 frames — that does not need the Prefect UI but does need structured terminal output so the user can see which frames have completed and which have failed.

Single render. After editing a recipe YAML and wanting to see the result immediately, the user runs a single render for today's date without triggering the full pipeline (no fetch, no process — just payload build and render). This is the tightest feedback loop in the creative workflow.

Recipe validation. Before committing a new recipe, the user validates it: schema check, region sanity check, source availability check. Currently no tooling exists for this.

Status inspection. The user wants to know: which recipes have been rendered, what date range each covers, what is missing, and when the last successful run was. This is a read-only operation that should work without the Prefect server running.

Production run management. The scheduled daily run goes through the Prefect server and is visible in the UI. The user may want to trigger an out-of-schedule run through the server (e.g. after a source was unavailable at 06:00 UTC and has since recovered) and have it appear in the run history alongside the scheduled runs.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
The CLI must:

  • provide a clean oc entry point usable from any terminal session, inside or outside Docker
  • cover the full operational surface: run, backfill, render, status, recipes, index
  • distinguish between direct invocation (always available, terminal output) and server dispatch (requires running stack, UI visibility) via an explicit --via-server flag
  • stream structured progress to the terminal for long-running operations (backfill)
  • work without the Prefect server running for all read operations and direct-invocation commands
  • integrate cleanly with RFC-008's backfill flow — oc backfill is the primary entry point for historical renders
  • be declared in pyproject.toml as a console script so oc is available as a command after pip install -e .

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
From OC-04 §constraints and §deployment:

  • The pipeline container runs Python + Node + Chromium inside Docker Compose. The CLI runs inside this container; docker compose exec pipeline oc <command> is the invocation pattern from the host.
  • Alternatively, if the CLI is installed in the host Python environment (outside Docker), it can connect to the pipeline's file system via mounted volumes. This is a secondary invocation path, not the primary.
  • Prefect Server runs as a separate container in Docker Compose at localhost:4200. It is always running when the stack is up but must not be a hard dependency for direct-invocation commands.
  • CLAUDE.md must document which entry point is canonical for each use case (scheduled run vs ad-hoc run vs backfill) to prevent two invocation paths producing divergent pipeline state.

docs.title: Proposed approach
docs.text: A typer-based CLI installed as the oc console script. Two invocation modes unified under one interface, with --via-server as the explicit switch for server dispatch. The scheduled daily run continues to go through the Prefect server as before — the CLI does not replace or interfere with it.

docs.title: Command surface
docs.text:
oc run [--via-server] [--dry-run]
    Run the full daily pipeline for today's date.
    Default: direct invocation, output to terminal.
    --via-server: dispatch to Prefect Server, visible in UI at localhost:4200.
    --dry-run: resolve what would run without executing.

oc backfill --recipe NAME --from DATE [--to DATE] [--cadence monthly|daily]
             [--concurrency N] [--via-server]
    Run the backfill flow for a recipe over a date range.
    DATE format: YYYY-MM or YYYY-MM-DD depending on cadence.
    --concurrency: override RENDER_CONCURRENCY for this run (RFC-008).
    Streams per-frame progress to terminal. Skips already-rendered dates
    (Prefect cache). Exits with non-zero code if any frame fails.

oc render --recipe NAME --date DATE [--force]
    Render a single frame. Skips fetch and process — reads from
    data/processed/ directly. Fails fast if processed data is absent.
    --force: re-render even if renders/{recipe}/{date}.png exists.
    Primary use: recipe iteration during the creative loop.

oc status [--recipe NAME]
    Print pipeline state to terminal. No server required.
    Without --recipe: summary table of all recipes — frame count,
    date range, last render timestamp, missing-date gaps.
    With --recipe: detailed view for one recipe including gap analysis.

oc recipes list
    List all recipes in recipes/ with name, render type, source, region.

oc recipes validate NAME
    Validate a recipe YAML: schema check, lat/lon sanity, source
    availability check against data/processed/. Prints pass/fail per check.

oc index --rebuild
    Manually rebuild manifest.json from renders/. Idempotent.
    Use after manual file operations or a partial pipeline failure.

docs.title: Invocation modes in detail
docs.text:
Direct invocation (default)

oc run calls daily_pipeline() as a plain Python function. Prefect's @task decorator still applies — result caching operates on the filesystem (data/processed/ cache hits are respected), retries fire on network failure. The run does not appear in the Prefect UI. Output is streamed to the terminal with a simple structured format:

[06:14:22] discover     ✓  3 sources · latest dates resolved
[06:14:23] fetch        ✓  oisst 2026-04-30 · 2.1 MB
[06:14:31] process      ✓  oisst · 720×1440 grid · 0.3% NaN
[06:14:31] build        ✓  north_atlantic_sst · gulf_stream_thermal
[06:14:33] render       ✓  north_atlantic_sst · 11.2s
[06:14:46] render       ✓  gulf_stream_thermal · 13.0s
[06:14:46] index        ✓  manifest.json · 2 recipes · 127 frames total

Server dispatch (--via-server)

oc run --via-server submits a flow run to the Prefect Server via the Prefect client API. The run appears in the UI at localhost:4200, is logged to Prefect's database, and contributes to run history alongside the scheduled daily runs. The terminal shows the flow run ID and a URL to the run detail page:

Flow run submitted → http://localhost:4200/flow-runs/abc123
Watching run... (Ctrl+C to detach, run continues in server)
[06:14:22] discover  ✓
[06:14:31] fetch     ✓
...

Scheduled daily run (unchanged)

The 06:00 UTC daily run is declared as a Prefect deployment in Docker Compose. It goes through the server, appears in run history, and is unaffected by the CLI. This is the production path. The CLI's oc run --via-server produces an identical run via the same deployment, just triggered on demand rather than on schedule.

docs.title: Terminal output for backfill
docs.text:
Backfill is the only long-running command. Progress is streamed as a compact per-frame line, with a live summary bar:

oc backfill --recipe gulf_stream_thermal --from 1981-01 --cadence monthly

Backfill · gulf_stream_thermal · 1981-01 → 2026-04 · 544 frames · concurrency 6

[████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░] 203/544  37%  ~18m remaining

✓ 1981-01   8.9s      ✓ 1981-02   9.1s      ✓ 1981-03  11.2s
✓ 1981-04   9.8s      ✓ 1981-05   8.7s      ✗ 1981-06  FAILED
✓ 1981-07  10.1s      ...

Completed: 202   Failed: 1   Skipped (cached): 0
Failed dates: 1981-06  →  re-run with: oc render --recipe gulf_stream_thermal --date 1981-06-01

Failed frames are surfaced immediately with the exact re-run command, so fixing a single failed render requires one follow-up invocation rather than re-running the entire backfill.

docs.title: Library and installation
docs.text:
typer with rich output (rich library for progress bars and coloured terminal output). Declared in pyproject.toml:

[project.scripts]
oc = "oceancanvas.cli:app"

[project.optional-dependencies]
cli = ["typer[all]>=0.12", "rich>=13"]

Installed inside the pipeline container at build time. Available on the host if the project is installed with pip install -e ".[cli]".

docs.title: Alternatives considered
docs.text:
Makefile targets instead of a CLI. make run, make backfill DATE=1981-01 etc. Simple, zero dependencies. Rejected: Makefiles do not compose well (no --help, no argument validation, no progress streaming), are awkward to call from inside Docker, and produce no structured output. A CLI is the right abstraction at this project's complexity level.

Prefect's own CLI (prefect run flow --name ...). Prefect ships a CLI that can trigger flow runs against the server. Rejected as the primary interface: it requires the server to be running for all invocations, has no knowledge of OceanCanvas-specific concepts (recipes, backfill cadence, gap analysis), and cannot do direct invocation. It remains available as a power-user escape hatch.

Click instead of typer. Click is the underlying library typer wraps. Rejected in favour of typer: typer's type-annotation-driven argument parsing eliminates boilerplate, produces better --help output automatically, and handles the --via-server flag pattern cleanly. The only cost is a thin wrapper dependency; acceptable.

Always dispatch through the server (no direct invocation). Simpler mental model — one invocation path, everything visible in the UI. Rejected: this makes the backfill and single-render commands dependent on the Prefect server being healthy, adds latency to every invocation, and means oc render --recipe X --date Y cannot be used when iterating quickly on a recipe with the full Docker stack down. Direct invocation must be the default.

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
Direct invocation bypasses Prefect Server's run database. Ad-hoc oc run and oc backfill executions are not recorded in the UI's run history. This is acceptable because the primary historical record — the daily production run — goes through the server. Ad-hoc runs are operational actions, not production events.

Two invocation paths (direct and server dispatch) must produce identical output — the same PNGs in the same locations, the same manifest.json. The risk is drift: a bug in the server-dispatch path that does not appear in direct invocation, or vice versa. Mitigation: both paths call the same Prefect flow functions. The only difference is the Python call site. The e2e CI test (GitHub Actions) runs in direct invocation mode; a separate smoke test should confirm server dispatch reaches the same result.

The --via-server flag name must be documented clearly in CLAUDE.md to prevent a future developer from treating direct invocation as the "real" pipeline and server dispatch as an optional extra. The production daily run goes through the server; the flag exists to bring ad-hoc runs into that same observable record when needed.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
1. Host vs container installation. Should oc be installable on the host (outside Docker) and connect to the pipeline's volumes via mounts? This would allow oc status without docker compose exec. The volume mount paths would need to be configurable. Defer to implementation — start with container-only, add host support if it proves useful.

  2. oc render and missing processed data. If oc render --recipe X --date 1985-03 is called but data/processed/oisst/1985-03-01.json does not exist, the command must fail clearly. Should it offer to fetch-and-process first, or fail-fast and tell the user to run oc run --date 1985-03 first? Lean toward fail-fast with a clear message — auto-fetching in a render command is surprising behaviour.

  3. oc status gap analysis. Detecting missing dates requires knowing the expected date range per recipe. The recipe YAML does not currently declare a start_date. Should it? Without it, gap analysis can only infer the expected range from the first and last rendered frames, which is approximate. This may be a recipe schema question (RFC-001).

  4. Colour and rich output in Docker logs. rich output with ANSI colour codes looks correct in an interactive terminal but produces noise in Docker log output. The CLI should detect TTY attachment and fall back to plain text in non-interactive contexts (e.g. when oc run is called from a cron job or CI).

docs.title: How this closes
docs.text:
ADR-NNN — CLI entry point and invocation model. Locks typer as the CLI library, oc as the console script name, and the direct/server-dispatch dual-mode design. Closes once the command surface is agreed and the pyproject.toml entry is committed.

ADR-NNN — Prefect server role clarification. Locks the definition: Prefect Server handles the scheduled daily run and server-dispatch invocations; direct invocation is the default for all ad-hoc CLI commands. Updates CLAUDE.md with the canonical entry point per use case. Closes alongside the ADR above.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• OC-04 Technical Architecture — §six-pipeline-tasks, §deployment, §tech-stack
  • RFC-008 — Pipeline Parallelisation Strategy (backfill flow, RENDER_CONCURRENCY)
  • RFC-001 — Recipe YAML schema (open question 3: start_date field)
  • Prefect docs — flow invocation without server, client API for server dispatch
  • typer docs — console scripts, argument validation, rich integration

docs.title: RFC-010 — Generative Audio Composition
docs.text: Status · Decided · closes into ADR-027 TA anchor · §components/render-system · §contracts (audio scalars in payload) Related · PRD-005 Video Editor · ADR-026 Audio stem system · ADR-024 Key moment detection Closes into · ADR-027 (supersedes ADR-026) Why this is an RFC · ADR-026 shipped a stem-switching system that loops a single audio file for long stretches. The result is ambient wallpaper, not data-driven composition. The audio doesn't follow the video — it sits beside it. This RFC proposes a multi-layer generative approach where the data performs the music, borrowing from electronic composition techniques. The architecture is genuinely open: multiple plausible layer designs, synthesis methods, and mapping strategies exist.

docs.title: The question
docs.text:
How do we make audio that follows the data the way the video does — where a viewer feels the music respond to what they see, not merely accompany it?

The current stem system (ADR-026) crossfades between 5 intensity levels based on a per-frame intensity signal. In practice, SST data changes slowly across 534 monthly frames. One stem loops for hundreds of frames. The audio is static, decorative, disconnected from the visual rhythm.

The question is not whether audio should react to data — that's decided (PRD-005 sharpest threat). The question is how: what layers of audio respond to what aspects of the data, at what timescales, using what synthesis techniques.

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
Borrowed from electronic composition and generative music:

  1. Multiple timescales. A composition has layers operating at different speeds — a pad evolving over minutes, a rhythm cycling every few seconds, accents firing on events. One data signal driving one audio parameter produces monotony. Multiple mappings at multiple speeds produce composition.

  2. The data performs. The data values are not metadata about the music — they are the music's performer. Temperature maps to pitch. Rate of change maps to rhythm. Records trigger accents. The mapping is direct, not decorative.

  3. Tension and release. Rising data should build tension (denser texture, higher pitch, faster pulse). Records and peaks are release points (accent + brief silence or drop). Falling data should relax (sparser, lower, slower). This is the emotional grammar that makes a viewer feel the connection.

  4. Surprise from the data, not the system. The audio system is deterministic. Same data = same composition. Surprises come from the data's own structure — an unexpected spike, a record-breaking month, a sudden reversal. The system reveals these; it doesn't create them.

docs.title: Layer 1 — Drone (timescale: entire timeline)
docs.text:
A continuous tonal layer whose pitch and brightness follow the data value.

Mapping: - Data value (e.g., SST mean) → oscillator frequency - Range: 80 Hz (coldest) → 400 Hz (hottest) - Filter cutoff follows value — cold = dark/muffled, hot = bright/open - Slow portamento (glide between frequencies over ~500ms)

Synthesis: Web Audio OscillatorNode (sawtooth, filtered) or pre-rendered drone stems pitch-shifted via playbackRate.

What the viewer hears: A low hum that rises and falls with the temperature. Over 45 years, the drone drifts upward as the ocean warms. The warming trend becomes audible.

docs.title: Layer 2 — Pulse (timescale: frame-to-frame change)
docs.text:
A rhythmic element whose speed and intensity follow the rate of change between consecutive frames.

Mapping: - |value[i] - value[i-1]| → pulse rate (BPM) - Stable periods (small delta): slow pulse, quiet (60 BPM, volume 0.1) - Volatile periods (large delta): fast pulse, loud (180 BPM, volume 0.7) - Direction of change: rising = higher click pitch, falling = lower click pitch

Synthesis: Short percussive samples (click, tick, drop) triggered at computed intervals via setTimeout or scheduled AudioBufferSourceNode. Three samples: tick-up, tick-neutral, tick-down.

What the viewer hears: A gentle heartbeat that quickens during El Niño events and calms during stable decades. The pulse literally accelerates when the data gets volatile.

docs.title: Layer 3 — Accent (timescale: key moments only)
docs.text:
Short, distinctive sounds fired at key moments — record highs, record lows, inflection points.

Mapping: - Record high: ascending chime (3 notes up) - Record low: descending tone (3 notes down) - Inflection point (trend reversal): single bell tone - Each accent plays once per event, not sustained

Synthesis: Pre-rendered samples (~1-2 seconds each). Three audio files: - accent_record_high.mp3 - accent_record_low.mp3 - accent_inflection.mp3

What the viewer hears: Sparse, memorable moments — a chime rings when 2023 sets the all-time SST record. The viewer feels "something just happened" before they consciously read the data.

docs.title: Layer 4 — Texture (timescale: seasonal/cyclical)
docs.text:
An ambient layer that responds to the seasonal cycle and absolute position in the timeline.

Mapping: - Month of year → texture type (winter = sparse/crystalline, summer = full/warm) - Position in timeline (0% → 100%) → overall density buildup - The texture grows denser as the timeline progresses, reflecting accumulation

Synthesis: Filtered noise with seasonal modulation. Brown noise for winter (low-pass at 200 Hz), pink noise for summer (low-pass at 800 Hz). Volume envelope follows a sine wave at 12-frame period (annual cycle).

What the viewer hears: An oceanic breathing rhythm — sparse and cold in winter months, fuller in summer — that gradually intensifies over the decades.

docs.title: Browser-side (preview playback)
docs.text:
All four layers synthesised in real-time using Web Audio API:

AudioContext
├── Layer 1: OscillatorNode → BiquadFilterNode → GainNode → destination
├── Layer 2: AudioBufferSourceNode (scheduled) → GainNode → destination
├── Layer 3: AudioBufferSourceNode (triggered) → GainNode → destination
└── Layer 4: AudioBufferSourceNode (noise loop) → BiquadFilterNode → GainNode → destination

Master gain node for overall volume control. Each layer has its own gain for independent mixing.

Pre-rendered assets needed: - 3 pulse samples: tick-up.mp3, tick-neutral.mp3, tick-down.mp3 (~0.1s each) - 3 accent samples: record-high.mp3, record-low.mp3, inflection.mp3 (~1.5s each) - 1 noise loop: texture-base.mp3 (~10s, looped)

Total: ~500KB of audio assets.

docs.title: Server-side (export)
docs.text:
For MP4 export, the four layers are rendered to separate WAV tracks via ffmpeg synthesis, then mixed:

  1. Drone: ffmpeg -f lavfi -i "sine=f={freq}:d={duration}" with frequency automation
  2. Pulse: Concatenated tick samples at computed intervals
  3. Accent: Samples placed at event frame times
  4. Texture: Filtered noise with seasonal envelope

Mixed via amix filter into single AAC track in MP4.

docs.title: Data contract
docs.text:
The generative mixer reads: - values[]: per-frame data values (from time-series JSON) - dates[]: per-frame dates (for seasonal extraction) - events[]: key moment events (from detectMoments()) - fps: playback rate (determines real-time mapping)

All available in the Video Editor component already.

docs.title: Recipe as audio recipe
docs.text: The recipe YAML already defines visual character (colormap, opacity, creative state). It should equally define audio character. The creative-to-technical mapping (ADR-018, ADR-021) extends naturally to audio:

docs.title: Creative state → audio parameters
docs.text: Creative control Visual effect Audio effect colour_character (0=arctic, 1=otherworldly) Palette selection Drone waveform: 0=sine (pure), 0.5=triangle, 1=sawtooth (rich harmonics) temporal_weight (0=moment, 1=epoch) Tail length, contrast Drone portamento: 0=instant pitch changes, 1=very slow glide energy_x (0=calm, 1=turbulent) Speed, smooth/rough Pulse sensitivity: 0=very subtle pulse, 1=aggressive rhythmic response energy_y (0=ghost, 1=solid) Opacity, presence Overall audio volume/density: 0=barely audible, 1=full presence mood preset Combined visual state Combined audio character (see below)

docs.title: Mood → audio character
docs.text: Mood Drone Pulse Accent Texture Becalmed Sine, slow glide, quiet Very subtle, slow Soft chimes Sparse, crystalline Deep current Triangle, medium glide Moderate, steady Bell tones Warm, flowing Storm surge Sawtooth, fast changes Aggressive, loud Sharp accents Dense, turbulent Surface shimmer Triangle, medium Light, dancing Bright pings Airy, shimmering Arctic still Sine, very slow Near-silent Deep, resonant Icy, minimal

docs.title: Recipe YAML extension
docs.text:
# ⊓ creative controls ⊓
render:
  type: field
  colormap: thermal
  opacity: 0.8

audio:
  drone_waveform: triangle    # from colour_character
  drone_glide: 0.7            # from temporal_weight
  pulse_sensitivity: 0.3      # from energy_x
  presence: 0.6               # from energy_y
  accent_style: chime          # chime | bell | ping | drop
  texture_density: 0.4        # seasonal texture amount

The Recipe Editor's creative controls would generate both render: and audio: blocks from the same mood/slider state. The YAML round-trip (ADR-021) extends to include audio parameters.

docs.title: Alternatives considered
docs.text:
Keep stem crossfading (ADR-026). Simpler, already working. Rejected because it doesn't follow the data — stems loop statically for long periods. The audio is ambient decoration, not composition.

Full MIDI synthesis. Map data to MIDI events, render via SoundFont or synth engine. Rejected as overkill for v1 — requires a full synthesis engine, large sample libraries, and complex scheduling. The four-layer approach achieves similar expressiveness with minimal assets.

Commercial generative music API (Mubert, Beatoven). Generate per-export tracks via API call. Rejected for Phase 1 — adds API dependency, cost, and non-determinism. Revisit as Phase 2 enhancement for users who want more polished output.

Single oscillator mapped to data. The simplest possible approach — one tone that follows the data value. Rejected because it produces a whine, not music. Multiple layers at different timescales are what make it feel composed.

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
Complexity. Four layers with independent scheduling is more complex than stem switching. Mitigation: each layer is independent and can be developed/tested separately.

Determinism. Web Audio scheduling is inherently non-deterministic (timing depends on system load). Mitigation: for export, use ffmpeg offline rendering which is deterministic. Browser preview is "close enough" — same composition, slightly different timing.

Asset size. ~500KB of audio samples. Acceptable — smaller than the current stem system (3.5MB for 3 themes × 5 stems).

Browser compatibility. Web Audio API is supported in all modern browsers. OscillatorNode, BiquadFilterNode, and AudioBufferSourceNode are baseline features.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
1. Drone synthesis: oscillator vs. pitch-shifted sample? Oscillator is smaller and more flexible but sounds synthetic. A pitch-shifted recording of a real instrument (bowed bass, organ drone) sounds richer but requires larger assets. Start with oscillator, upgrade if it sounds too digital.

  2. Pulse sample design. What do the tick samples sound like? Water drops? Sonar pings? Synthesised clicks? This is an artistic choice that sets the emotional register. Propose: start with simple filtered clicks, iterate based on listening.

  3. Layer mixing ratios. Default volumes for each layer? Propose: Drone 0.4, Pulse 0.3, Accent 0.5 (louder but sparse), Texture 0.2. User-adjustable per-layer volume in the UI.

  4. Seasonal texture vs. data-driven texture. Layer 4 uses month-of-year for seasonal variation. Alternative: use the data's own variance (rolling standard deviation) instead. The seasonal approach is more predictable; the variance approach is more data-driven.

  5. Export determinism. Can ffmpeg produce byte-identical audio from the same inputs? Need to verify with a determinism test. If not, accept "perceptually identical" for audio (visual renders are already byte-identical).

docs.title: How this closes
docs.text: ADR-027 — Generative audio composition. Locks the four-layer architecture, Web Audio implementation for preview, ffmpeg rendering for export. Supersedes ADR-026 (stem switching) for the generative portion; ADR-026 remains as the fallback for offline/simple exports.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• PRD-005 — Video Editor (sharpest threat: audio reads as decorative)
  • ADR-024 — Key moment detection (provides events for Layer 3)
  • ADR-026 — Audio stem system (current implementation, to be superseded)
  • RFC-007 — Key moment detection (per-frame intensity signal)
  • Web Audio API specification — OscillatorNode, BiquadFilterNode

docs.title: RFC-011 — Tension arc as shared primitive
docs.text: Status · Decided · closes into ADR-028 TA anchor · §components/render-system · §contracts/recipe-yaml · §contracts/render-payload · §constraints Related · PRD-006 The piece · ADR-027 Generative audio composition · ADR-008 Shared payload format · ADR-024 Key moment detection Closes into · ADR-028 (locks the arc representation, expansion logic, and consumer protocol) Why this is an RFC · The tension arc has to be authored once in the recipe and consumed identically by three independent systems — the browser audio engines, the pipeline audio synthesis, and the ffmpeg filter graph that keys the visual filters. How it is represented in YAML, where it is expanded into a per-frame array, and how that array reaches each consumer all have plausible alternatives with real trade-offs.

docs.title: The question
docs.text:
PRD-006 calls for a single authored curve — a tension arc — that shapes audio dynamics and visual filter values in unison across the duration of a video. The curve is the load-bearing primitive: pad amplitude, sequence density, ffmpeg eq=saturation, vignette intensity, and rate-of-playback at flagged key moments all derive from it.

The architectural question is how that one curve is authored, expressed, and distributed so the audio engine, the pipeline synthesis, and the ffmpeg filter graph all see the same shape without recomputing it differently. The arc has to round-trip cleanly through ADR-008's payload, has to be cheap to author (the editor needs live preview), and has to be deterministic (the same recipe + same date must produce the same arc array down to the float).

Three forces apply: the curve must be author-friendly (a recipe author should not write 534 floats), transmissible (it has to fit in YAML and survive the payload pipeline), and expansion-deterministic (TS and Python expansions must produce byte-identical arrays for parity).

docs.title: Use cases
docs.text:
1. Live editing. The author opens the Video Editor, drags an arc-peak handle, and immediately hears the audio rebalance and sees the visualizer overlay update. No pipeline round-trip; the browser computes the arc array on the fly.
  2. Pipeline export. oceancanvas export-video reads the recipe, expands the arc to a per-frame array, feeds it to audio.py for amplitude modulation and to video.py for ffmpeg filter keyframing. Same expansion logic as the browser; cross-validated.
  3. Key moment pinning. The dominant key moment from moments.py (the all-time record, typically) becomes a peak of the arc regardless of what the author chose, so a Record Moment hold lands on a real significant frame even when the arc preset is Drift or Plateau.
  4. Future consumers. Modal scales (PRD-007) will modulate density along the arc. Phase 4 video formats (ghost accumulation, temporal split) read the arc to drive opacity and split-line softness. The primitive has to support consumers that do not exist yet.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
• A single representation in recipe YAML — author-friendly enough to edit by hand, expressive enough to cover the four preset shapes named in the Creative doc (Classic / Plateau / Drift / Invert).
  • Deterministic expansion to a per-frame arc[]: number[0..1] array. Same recipe + same dates + same key moments → byte-identical arc array.
  • Cross-validation parity. TypeScript expansion (browser) and Python expansion (pipeline) produce identical arrays for every recipe.
  • One source of truth. The arc is computed once per consumer, not duplicated across audio.py and video.py and tensionArc.ts.
  • Cheap to interpolate. Audio modulates per-frame; ffmpeg keyframes at a sampled rate. Both have to be efficient.
  • Does not bloat the recipe. The arc adds at most a small typed block; not a 534-float array.
  • Forward-compatible. Future consumers (modal density, ghost opacity, temporal split softness) plug into the same array without schema changes.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• File-based YAML storage (TA §constraints). No database; arc parameters live in the recipe file alongside render: and audio:.
  • Shared payload format (ADR-008). The arc array has to round-trip through the render payload because the browser preview and the pipeline both need it; whatever shape the YAML stores must serialize cleanly into the payload.
  • Deterministic rendering (TA §constraints). Visual frames remain byte-identical for the same recipe + data + date; the arc must not introduce non-determinism. Audio is "perceptually identical" per ADR-027.
  • Daily cadence (TA §constraints). Expansion must be cheap enough to run as part of the once-daily pipeline render without measurable overhead.
  • Self-hostable (TA §constraints). No remote curve-rendering services; expansion happens locally in the pipeline and the browser.

docs.title: Recipe schema — preset + parameters
docs.text:
The recipe stores a typed tension_arc: block with a preset name and a small number of shaping parameters:

# ⊓ creative controls ⊓
render:
  type: field
  colormap: thermal
  ...
audio:
  drone_waveform: triangle
  ...
tension_arc:
  preset: classic           # classic | plateau | drift | invert | none
  peak_position: 0.65       # 0–1 fraction of duration where the arc peaks
  peak_height: 1.0          # 0–1 maximum value the arc reaches
  release_steepness: 0.7    # 0–1 how sharply the arc drops after the peak
  pin_key_moment: true      # if true, peak position is overridden to align with the dominant key moment frame

preset: none opts out — the arc array is constant 1.0 across the duration, leaving v0.4.0's flat dynamics behaviour as the default for recipes that do not author one.

docs.title: Shared expansion logic — TS ↔ Py parity
docs.text:
A tensionArc.ts module in the gallery and an arc.py module in the pipeline both implement the same function:

expandArc(spec: TensionArcSpec, totalFrames: int, dominantMomentFrame: int | null) → number[totalFrames]

Both modules consume the same fixture file in tests/cross-validation/tension-arc/ to assert byte-identical outputs for a battery of (preset, peak_position, peak_height, release_steepness, totalFrames, momentFrame) tuples. Same pattern as creative_mapping_fixtures.json for the audio mapping cross-validation that already ships in v0.4.0.

The four preset shapes are defined as parametric curves over t ∈ [0, 1]:

  • Classic — pow(t/peak_position, 2) * peak_height for t ≤ peak_position; cubic ease-out scaled by release_steepness for t > peak_position.
  • Plateau — early ramp, sustained plateau at peak_height, late drop. Two parameters (ramp duration, drop steepness) collapse onto peak_position and release_steepness.
  • Drift — cosine wave around peak_height * 0.5 with low frequency; no clear peak. release_steepness controls wave amplitude.
  • Invert — Classic mirrored around the time axis. Starts tense, releases early.

When pin_key_moment is true and a dominant moment exists (highest-scoring event from detect_moments), the curve is recomputed with peak_position set to momentFrame / totalFrames. The peak is guaranteed to land on a real significant frame.

docs.title: Distribution — payload field
docs.text:
The expanded arc[] array travels through the render payload as a new optional field:

{
  "version": 2,
  "recipe": {
    "id": "...",
    "audio": { ... },
    "tension_arc": [0.0, 0.02, 0.05, ..., 0.97, 0.94, ...]
  },
  ...
}

Pipeline build_payload task computes the arc once and serialises it. Browser payloadBuilder.ts mirrors. Audio engines and the ffmpeg filter graph both read the field, never re-expand.

docs.title: Audio engine consumption
docs.text: Both SynthEngine and AmbientEngine gain a setTensionArc(arc: number[]) → void method on the existing AudioEngineInterface. Per-frame layer gains multiply by arc[frame] alongside the existing channelScale(mix, ...) and mix.master multipliers. Identical contract on both engines; downstream ToneEngine (if it ever ships) implements the same.

docs.title: ffmpeg filter graph consumption
docs.text: The pipeline video.py assemble_video step samples the arc at 1Hz (one keypoint per second of output, regardless of fps) and emits eq=saturation=expr and vignette=PI/4*expr filter expressions with linear interpolation between keypoints. Rate slowdown via setpts triggered at the dominant moment frame: a 1-second setpts=4*PTS segment, framed by short ramps in and out. A 30s video gets ~30 keypoints; a 45s SST timelapse gets ~45 keypoints. Filter graph length stays bounded.

docs.title: How this closes
docs.text: ADR-028 once Phase 1 implementation lands and the schema is locked. The ADR will name: the four preset shapes, the parameter ranges, the pin_key_moment semantics, the payload field name, and the engine interface signature.

docs.title: Alternative — raw per-frame array in YAML
docs.text:
Recipe stores tension_arc: [0.0, 0.02, 0.05, ...] as a literal array of 365–534 floats. Expansion is identity.

Rejected. Authoring becomes hostile (no one hand-edits a 534-element list), version control diffs balloon, the arc cannot be parametrically tweaked from the editor without rewriting the entire array. The compactness goal collides with the author-friendliness goal.

docs.title: Alternative — control-points expanded only at runtime
docs.text:
Recipe stores N control points; expansion happens lazily at consumer time. The browser audio engine expands. The pipeline expands separately. ffmpeg gets keyframes from the pipeline expansion.

Rejected. Two consumers expanding independently is exactly the duplicated-curve risk the proposal exists to defeat. Even with shared cross-validated logic, expansion at the consumer is an integrity hazard if one consumer forgets to pin to the same key-moment frame the other did. Single-expansion-into-payload is the cleaner contract.

docs.title: Alternative — pipeline-only computation, payload-distributed
docs.text:
Pipeline expands the arc and writes it to the payload. Browser preview reads the payload. No expansion logic in the browser at all.

Rejected. The Recipe Editor and Video Editor both need live arc preview as the author drags the peak handle. A pipeline round-trip per slider tick is unworkable. The expansion has to live on both sides; cross-validation is the safety net.

docs.title: Alternative — Bezier curve with handles
docs.text:
Recipe stores Bezier control points; the editor exposes draggable handles for the curve.

Rejected for v1. Bezier handles are notoriously fiddly UI; the four named presets cover the cases the Creative doc identifies. If authors hit the expressive ceiling of preset-plus-three-parameters, a future RFC can introduce a preset: custom extension with explicit control points.

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
• Recipe schema grows. The tension_arc: block is one new top-level key under the creative-controls marker. Acceptable; matches the precedent set by audio: in v0.4.0.
  • Payload v2. The arc becomes a payload field, bumping payload version. Existing v1 payloads continue to work; consumers default to constant 1.0 if the field is missing.
  • ffmpeg keypoint density. 1Hz sampling caps complexity, but on long-duration timelapses (multi-minute) the filter graph still grows linearly. Acceptable for v1; if it becomes a bottleneck, the fallback is sendcmd reading from a sidecar file.
  • Cross-validation overhead. Each new preset shape doubles the fixture surface. Manageable while there are four; revisit if the count exceeds eight.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
• Tone.js migration. Orthogonal. The arc primitive works against any synthesis engine that implements setTensionArc. Decided in a separate RFC if and when the synthesis ceiling is reached.
  • Arc preset count. Four shapes (Classic / Plateau / Drift / Invert) match the Creative doc's enumeration. If recipe authors consistently want a fifth shape, it is a one-line addition to the expansion module plus a fixture row.
  • Modal scales as a separate RFC. Recommended. Modal scales modulate pitch space; the arc modulates dynamics. Different concerns, different mappings, different user-facing UI. RFC-012 candidate.
  • Visual-side filters beyond eq and vignette. LFO-driven vignette pulse, mode-driven LUT, grain-driven jitter all layer onto the arc primitive. Each is a small follow-up; none belong in this RFC.

docs.title: How this closes
docs.text: ADR-028 — Tension arc as shared primitive. Locks the preset-plus-parameters YAML schema, the cross-validated expansion logic, the payload-field distribution, the audio engine interface signature, and the ffmpeg keypoint-sampling protocol. Closes once a Phase 1 implementation forces the schema decision (likely once the editor and the pipeline both read and consume the array end-to-end).
